source.title: Two education credits, one tuition bill: how to pick AOTC vs Lifetime Learning

ad_headlines: Two legal education credits. One tuition bill. Pick the better one per student.
AOTC vs. Lifetime Learning: same form, very different math.
Form 8863 holds both credits. The 1098-T tells you which one to take.
variants.twitter:
Two education credits, same Form 8863:
• AOTC — first 4 years of undergrad, partly refundable, generally larger
• LLC — undergrad, grad, job skills, no year limit, non-refundable, smaller

One per student per year. Source: IRC §25A, IRS Pub 970. Educational.
variants.linkedin:
If you paid tuition this year, the U.S. tax code gives you two competing credits — and you can only pick one per student per year.

Per IRC §25A and IRS Publication 970:

**American Opportunity Tax Credit (AOTC)**
• First several years of undergraduate study only.
• Partly refundable.
• Generally the more generous of the two.
• Additional rules apply on enrollment level and prior-year claims.

**Lifetime Learning Credit (LLC)**
• Covers undergraduate, graduate, and job-skill courses.
• No year limit per student.
• Non-refundable.
• Smaller credit amount.

Both phase out at higher incomes. The school reports qualifying tuition on Form 1098-T. You compute both credits and claim the better result on Form 8863, attached to Form 1040.

The honest move: run the numbers both ways, then pick. The 'better' credit isn't always AOTC — it depends on the student's year, your income, and the tuition figure on the 1098-T.

Educational only. Not tax advice — verify current-year income phase-outs and credit amounts against IRS guidance.
variants.reddit:
**AOTC vs. Lifetime Learning Credit — same tuition bill, two different credits**

Per IRC §25A and IRS Pub 970, you choose ONE per student per year.

**AOTC**
- First several years of undergrad only
- Partly refundable
- Generally the bigger credit
- Extra rules on enrollment + prior claims

**LLC**
- Undergrad, grad, job-skill courses
- No year limit
- Non-refundable
- Smaller credit amount

Both phase out at higher incomes. School issues Form 1098-T with qualifying tuition. You compute both, take the better one on Form 8863.

The 'better' credit depends on year-of-study, income, and the 1098-T number. Run both. Pick.

Sources: IRC §25A, IRS Pub 970, Form 8863. Educational only — not advice.

brand_voice_principles: Plain-spoken, confident, demystifying. Translates tax-code complexity.
A little irreverent about how needlessly complicated the system is — on the reader's side.
Specific and sourced. Every claim points to a real rule.
No: 'one weird trick', 'IRS hates this', guaranteed savings, sleaze.
